ending: The heist succeeds and the double-crosses resolve in layers. Nicodemus escapes with the Holy Grail - the thing he actually came for - but at ruinous cost: he sacrificed his daughter Deirdre at the Gate of Blood to reach it, his crew is dead or turned, his organization is gutted, and his defeat at the Carpenter house ends with his reputation - the thing a two-thousand-year-old schemer runs on - in ruins. Hannah Ascher, revealed as the host of Lasciel, dies in her fire-duel with Harry; the Genoskwa is dead; Goodman Grey stands revealed as Harry's own plant inside the crew, hired in advance for the price of one dollar. Murphy survives Nicodemus's trap, but Fidelacchius was broken when he goaded her into misusing it - until the assault on Michael's home, when Waldo Butters takes up the broken hilt and his faith ignites it into a blade of light: Butters is the new Knight of the Cross. Michael returns Uriel's borrowed Grace and goes back to his family at peace. The parasite in Harry's skull is delivered safely: not a killer but a child - Bonnie, a newborn spirit of intellect, daughter of Harry and the departed shadow of Lasciel - housed in a fresh skull in Harry's keeping. Harry walks away with a share of Hades' treasure, his life no longer under a deadline, and something better than gold: he goes to the Carpenters' door and properly meets his daughter Maggie, and decides to be her father. Mab, as always, has lost nothing: her debt is paid, and Nicodemus paid more.
in_short: Mab repays a debt to Nicodemus Archleone by loaning him Harry - as crew for a heist targeting the vault of Hades, lord of the underworld, to steal the Holy Grail. The crew: shapeshifter Goodman Grey, warlock Hannah Ascher and her partner Binder, the monstrous Genoskwa, thief Anna Valmont, Nicodemus's daughter Deirdre, and Murphy as Harry's eyes. Everyone plans to double-cross everyone; Harry, with Mab's tacit blessing, plans it best. Nicodemus goads Murphy into misusing Fidelacchius, breaking the sword and nearly killing her; Michael Carpenter leaves retirement to take her place, walking on Uriel's own borrowed Grace. The crew breaches the underworld through gates of Fire, Ice, and Blood - the last opened by Nicodemus sacrificing Deirdre. Ascher is unmasked as Lasciel's host and dies duelling Harry; Grey proves to be Harry's plant, hired for a dollar; Nicodemus flees with the Grail but is ruined at the Carpenter house, where Butters raises the broken sword, reignited by faith, and becomes the new Knight of the Cross. Harry's lethal head-parasite is born as Bonnie, a spirit-of-intellect daughter of Harry and Lash's shadow, and Harry finally meets Maggie and chooses to be her dad.

recaps.text: Previously in the series: Harry Dresden serves as the Winter Knight, mortal enforcer to Mab, Queen of Air and Darkness - the price he paid to save his daughter Maggie, who now lives safely with the family of Michael Carpenter, the retired Knight of the Cross. Harry destroyed the entire Red Court of vampires, arranged and survived his own assassination, and, on his first true assignment for Mab, killed Maeve, the Nemesis-corrupted Winter Lady, on Halloween night - a night that also cost the life of the Summer Lady and passed both mantles on: Harry's apprentice Molly Carpenter is now the Winter Lady, a fact her parents do not know. Harry also learned what he is warden of: Demonreach, the island prison built by Merlin for horrors, which the Outsiders' hidden contagion Nemesis tried to blow open. Old enemies remain. Chief among them: Nicodemus Archleone, leader of the Knights of the Blackened Denarius - hosts of fallen angels bound in thirty silver coins - who once nearly killed Harry and whose fallen angel Lasciel long ago left a shadow of herself in Harry's mind; that shadow, Lash, grew into something that loved him and sacrificed herself to save his life. Since then, something has been growing inside Harry's skull - a parasite, by Mab's account, which gives him blinding headaches and will eventually kill him. For the past year he has kept to his island, away from Murphy, Molly, Butters, Thomas, and everyone else the Winter Knight's enemies might touch.

Mab comes to Demonreach to collect. She owes a debt - and her payment is Harry. He is loaned, on Mab's word of honor, to Nicodemus Archleone, to serve as crew on a job of Nicodemus's choosing until it is complete; Harry may do nothing to betray the endeavor before then, or he makes a liar of the Queen of Air and Darkness. The job, laid out in a Chicago slaughterhouse: a heist. The target is not a bank but the vault of Hades, Greek lord of the underworld - reachable through a way that opens from the most secure private vault in Chicago, which happens to belong to the gangster baron John Marcone. The prize Nicodemus names is the Holy Grail. The crew he assembles is a bestiary of specialists: his daughter Deirdre, a blade-demon Denarian; Goodman Grey, a shapeshifter who can wear anyone's face; Hannah Ascher, a fugitive warlock with a genius for fire magic, and her partner Binder, whose talent is summoning squads of expendable creatures; and the Genoskwa, a huge Bigfoot-kin killer who conceives an immediate hatred of Harry. Harry extracts one concession: his own second, and brings in Murphy. Both of them understand the real game - everyone at that table intends to double-cross everyone else, and Harry's task is to survive the job, honor the letter of Mab's debt, and be the last betrayal standing. Meanwhile the parasite in his skull is running out of patience, and Mab has made its removal contingent on his success.

Preparation, in bad company. The crew fills out its mortal expertise by recruiting Anna Valmont, the world-class thief and safecracker who once crossed the Denarians over the Shroud of Turin and lost her whole crew for it - she signs on with open eyes and a professional's cold blood. The plan takes shape in stages: crack Marcone's underground vault by mortal and magical means, then survive what waits beyond it, where the way opens into the underworld and the true vault's defenses answer to a god. Harry works his own side of the board in stolen moments. Butters - who has spent a year watching Harry's absence and now watches the Winter Knight consorting with Denarians - voices what several of Harry's friends quietly fear: that the mantle is turning Harry into something else, and that his word may no longer be worth what it was. The mistrust stings worse than the headaches. Complications multiply from outside, too: Nicodemus's estranged wife and fellow Denarian, Tessa, is working actively to wreck the job for reasons Nicodemus declines to share, and her interference turns preparation into a running skirmish. Through it all Harry watches his crewmates - Grey's inhuman precision, Ascher's easy charm, the Genoskwa's patient menace - and catalogs which knives will be aimed at his back when the double-crossing starts.

The cost of the job starts arriving before the vault door is even reached. Tessa's sabotage forces improvisations - including the murder, ahead of the crew's arrival, of a key mortal gatekeeper whose role Grey must cover by wearing the dead man's life like a suit. Nicodemus, unhurried, folds every setback into the plan, and Harry accumulates evidence of what he already knew: to Nicodemus, every member of this crew is a tool with a disposal schedule. Harry's private war on the side intensifies. His nights are spent shoring up his real assets - Murphy at his side, quiet arrangements whose shape he keeps even from the reader of his own thoughts, and consultations with Bob about the thing in his skull, whose deadline is no longer comfortably abstract. The parasite's episodes now drop him mid-stride, and Mab's bargain - finish the job, then the matter can be dealt with - reads more and more like a rope she has tied between him and the finish line. Around the edges, the pieces Nicodemus actually cares about click into place: the timing, the vault schedule, the desecration needed to open the way below, and a role reserved for the holy sword Fidelacchius, which has waited in Murphy's keeping for years for a hand worthy to draw it.

Nicodemus springs the trap he has been building around Karrin Murphy since the day Harry brought her aboard. In a confrontation staged for exactly this purpose, he goads her - with cruelty calibrated to her angers and her griefs - into drawing Fidelacchius against him and striking in wrath, outside the strict terms under which the Sword of Faith protects its wielder. The blade's power departs; Nicodemus breaks the ancient sword, and then, nearly, its bearer, maiming Murphy before Harry can pull her out. One of the three swords of the Cross is shattered, Murphy is out of the fight with wrecked knee and arm, and Nicodemus has demonstrated, at negligible cost, exactly how far ahead of everyone he plays. Harry needs a replacement for the heist's endgame, and the answer walks out of retirement: Michael Carpenter. The archangel Uriel, whose accounts with Nicodemus run long, takes an extraordinary step - he lends Michael his own Grace, the essence of an archangel, to carry as protection; while Michael bears it, his broken body serves him again, and Uriel goes as vulnerable as a mortal man. Michael takes up Amoracchius, tells his wife what must be told, and joins the crew of monsters - the best man Harry knows walking willingly into a den of the damned, on the strength of a borrowed light and his own unbendable faith.

The heist goes loud. The crew hits Marcone's bank in full: Valmont defeats the mortal security, Ascher burns through the warding, Binder's creatures hold the perimeter against the response Marcone's people mount, and the crew descends into the vault where the way to the underworld opens. Beyond it wait the true defenses: three gates that answer only to their proper keys. The Gate of Fire parts for Ascher's talent; the Gate of Ice yields to the Winter Knight; and the Gate of Blood takes the payment Nicodemus planned from the beginning - a sacrifice of what he loves. He kills Deirdre. His daughter, his one genuine bond across two thousand years, gives herself to open the last gate, and Nicodemus spends her with his eyes open. Tessa's fury at the plan stands explained, far too late to matter. Inside, the vault of Hades is a treasury out of myth - and among its holdings, five relics of the Crucifixion, the Grail among them. There the betrayals begin in earnest: Nicodemus turns on the hirelings the moment their usefulness ends, and the crew's hidden allegiances start surfacing one by one. Harry, cut off from the others in the dark of the underworld, comes face to face with the vault's owner - Hades himself, who has been expecting him, and whose interest in this particular burglary is not at all what Nicodemus believes.

Hades, it turns out, keeps his vault the way he keeps his realm: exactly as intended. His fireside conversation with Harry reframes the whole burglary - the lord of the underworld is no one's victim, and worthy purposes have a way of being permitted. Harry returns to the fight knowing more than he did. The unmaskings come fast. Hannah Ascher is revealed as the host of Lasciel - the Temptress, the fallen angel whose shadow once lived in Harry's head - and the seducer's long game collapses into a duel of fire in the underworld's tunnels that only Harry walks away from. The Genoskwa shows its own coin-bearing colors and its long-promised intent to grind Harry to paste, and Goodman Grey - at the moment betrayal would profit him most - reveals the job he was actually hired for. Grey has been Harry's man from the start, retained before the heist ever began, his unbreakable professional ethic bought for a fee of exactly one dollar. The crew's survivors fight their way out of the underworld with the Grail in Nicodemus's hands and everything else in ruins around him, and Nicodemus does what wounded monsters do: he goes to hurt Harry where it is least defended. The endgame converges on the house of Michael Carpenter - where Maggie Dresden lives, where the angelic guard has been thinned by Michael's absence, and where Nicodemus intends to take payment in kind for Deirdre.

The battle of the Carpenter house ends the job's accounting. Nicodemus and his surviving forces assault the home; against them stand Harry, Michael carrying Uriel's Grace, and the unlikeliest reserves - including Waldo Butters, the powerless, terrified, utterly determined little medical examiner, who in the fight's crux takes up the broken hilt of Fidelacchius. His faith - not in God, but in the fight between right and wrong being worth making - ignites the shattered sword into a blade of pure light, and Butters becomes the new Knight of the Cross. The Genoskwa dies; Nicodemus's squires are broken; and Nicodemus himself, beaten and humiliated before witnesses, escapes with the Grail and nothing else - his daughter spent, his crew dead or turned, his aura of invincibility gone, and the terms of his defeat arranged so precisely that Mab's debt stands paid in full while her Knight stands victorious. Mab does not lose. The last threat resolves gentlest of all: the parasite in Harry's skull is no monster but a child - a spirit of intellect, daughter of Harry and the departed shadow of Lasciel, conceived in Lash's final act of love. She is delivered safely into a prepared skull and named Bonnie. Harry ends the book with treasure from a god's vault, his deadline lifted, Murphy mending, and one door left to walk through: the Carpenters', where he finally, properly, meets his daughter Maggie - and stays.
